coredata.dc:title: Lecithin sensitized thermo-sensitive niosome using NIR-carbon dots for breast cancer combined chemo-photothermal therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Khakbaz, Faeze
Mirzaei, Mohammad
Mahani, Mohamad
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a new class of thermo-sensitive niosome (TS-Nio) was synthesized using lecithin and NIR-carbon dots, for combined chemo-photothermal therapy. TS-Nio (200 nm) and noisome (240 nm) were synthesized using thin layer method and were stable for 14 days in water, buffer, and cellular environment. CQDs with a hydrodynamic diameter of 2.0 ± 0.5 nm (during 14 days) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method, and their photothermal, and quantum efficiency were 24.3% and 67%, respectively. Synthesized CQDs had repeating maximum temperature of 55 ± 0.2 °C in four 10-min irradiation cycles (LASER 808 nm) and photothermal therapy effect of 40%, 46%, 44%, and 48% for MCF-10A, MCF-7, MDA-MB-231, SKBR-3 cells, respectively. Drug and CQDs were loaded in the niosomes, and the release difference between normal and cancer cell conditions were 55% and 21% for TS-Nio and niosome, respectively. The drug release kinetic model was Komsmeyer & Peppas. Chemotherapy on MCF-7 cancer cells using Dox-loaded niosomes showed the maximum toxicity of 46% and 37% for TS-Nio and niosome, respectively. The chemophotothermal therapy on MCF-10A, MCF-7, MDA-MB-231, SKBR-3 cells using drug and CQDs loaded niosomes with 10 min laser irradiation were 40%, 88%, 89%, 15% for TS-Nio, and 17%, 61%, 67%, 29% for niosome respectively. The results showed perfect targeting and effective chemophotothermal therapy of TS-Nio on MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231 cell lines.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thermosensitive niosome
Lecithin
NIR-Carbon dot
Breast cancer
Chemo-photothermal therapy

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photothermal effect and in vitro release study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Size distribution of the CQDs synthesized with a 1:5 ratio of citric acid and urea by hydrothermal method using DLS method. (inset: average diameter of the prepared CQDs after 1,3,7, and 14 days (in different media), (b) HRTEM image of the CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the synthesized CQDs, urea, and citric acid.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs recorded at various excitation wavelengths from 300 to 360 nm, (b) excitation-emission spectra of CQDs recorded at 450 nm and 340 nm as emission and excitation wavelengths, respectively. Fluorescence spectra of CQDs in (c) different pH from 3 to 7 and, (d) different mediums (water, PBS, RPMI, and RPMI + 10% FBS).
Absorption spectra of CQDs synthesized using various citric acid: urea molar ratio and synthesis condition.

Photothermal performances of the CQDs; (a) temperature elevation by CQDs in distilled water as a function of laser irradiation time during 10 min; (b) photothermal effects of colloidal CQDs during LASER on/off cycles; (c) plot of cooling time versus –ln θ; and (d) photothermal stability of CQDs for four successive irradiations and cooling cycles.
Optical microscopic image of (a) niosome and (b) thermo-sensitive noisome.
Drug release kinetics at different pH and temperature.
Cell viability of four different concentrations of various formulations.
The cell viability of photothermal therapy and combined therapy after incubation with various formulations with NIR laser irradiation.

Morphology of MDA-MB-231 cells after incubation with (a) high glucose DMEM, (b) high glucose DMEM with NIR laser irradiation, (c) CQDs, (d) CQDs with NIR laser irradiation,(e) CQD & Dox @ TS-Nio, and (f) CQD & Dox @ TS-Nio with NIR laser irradiation.
Hydrodynamic diameter (in nm) of niosome and thermo-sensitive niosome in various environments during 14 days.
Kinetic models of Dox release from prepared formulations in various conditions.
Calculated IC50 of various formulations against MCF-7 cell line.
IC50 (mM) under different treatments on MCF-7, MDA-MB-231, and SKBR-3 cells and the calculated CI.

head.ce:title: Lecithin sensitized thermo-sensitive niosome using NIR-carbon dots for breast cancer combined chemo-photothermal therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shahid Bahonar University of Kerman, Kerman, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Shahid Bahonar University of Kerman, Kerman, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman 7631818356, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman 7631818356, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a new class of thermo-sensitive niosome (TS-Nio) was synthesized using lecithin and NIR-carbon dots, for combined chemo-photothermal therapy. TS-Nio (200 nm) and noisome (240 nm) were synthesized using thin layer method and were stable for 14 days in water, buffer, and cellular environment. CQDs with a hydrodynamic diameter of 2.0 ± 0.5 nm (during 14 days) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method, and their photothermal, and quantum efficiency were 24.3% and 67%, respectively. Synthesized CQDs had repeating maximum temperature of 55 ± 0.2 °C in four 10-min irradiation cycles (LASER 808 nm) and photothermal therapy effect of 40%, 46%, 44%, and 48% for MCF-10A, MCF-7, MDA-MB-231, SKBR-3 cells, respectively. Drug and CQDs were loaded in the niosomes, and the release difference between normal and cancer cell conditions were 55% and 21% for TS-Nio and niosome, respectively. The drug release kinetic model was Komsmeyer & Peppas. Chemotherapy on MCF-7 cancer cells using Dox-loaded niosomes showed the maximum toxicity of 46% and 37% for TS-Nio and niosome, respectively. The chemophotothermal therapy on MCF-10A, MCF-7, MDA-MB-231, SKBR-3 cells using drug and CQDs loaded niosomes with 10 min laser irradiation were 40%, 88%, 89%, 15% for TS-Nio, and 17%, 61%, 67%, 29% for niosome respectively. The results showed perfect targeting and effective chemophotothermal therapy of TS-Nio on MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231 cell lines.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite all significant efforts made of prevention, understanding, early detection, and treatment of cancer, it is one of the most serious threats to human health [1–4]. However, traditional treatment routes such as surgery, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy have been widely applied in nowadays treatments. Unfortunately, chemotherapy as an important strategy in clinical practices has shown significant side effects such as drug resistance over time and damage to normal cells and tissues. Photothermal therapy (PTT), as a developing tumor treatment, can be used to reduce normal cells and tissue damage and overcome drug resistance [5–9]. PTT has been applied as a safe, non-invasive, effective, and selective method for local treatment of cancer site without any affecting on normal tissues. Photothermal agents such as organic polymers [10,11], noble metals [12–14], semiconductor nanoparticles [15,16], and carbon-based materials [17–19] are used to increase the light absorption and enhance the PTT efficacy. Among these, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are one of the best PT agents due to their impressive properties such as cheapness and availability, biocompatibility, stability, and high photothermal conversion capability. These zero-dimensional crystals trigger the absorbed light energy into heat or the liberation of stimulated photons and have been widely applied in bioimaging and PTT [20–24]. Unfortunately, PTT as a single treatment cannot eradicate cancerous cells completely, leading to cancer recurrence [25]. The failure of single rout PTT treatment, along with increasing the effectiveness of chemotherapy with heat, has led researchers to investigate the feasibility of combination therapy [26–32]. The benefits of nanomedical methods such as tumor accumulation improvement, pharmacokinetic due to enhanced permeability and retention effect, pharmawidely improvement due to enhanced delivery efficacy of targeted-nanocarriers, reduction in non-specific accumulation, the possibility of two or more treatments combined to achieve enhanced effectiveness by circumventing limitations of monotherapy, and definition of accurate tumor location and timely feedback by integrating imaging techniques can be used to overcome therapeutic limitations and increase the effectiveness. A multifunctional nanocarrier is essential to integrate these strategies into a single effective platform to maximize therapeutic efficacy [33–37].

Various nanocarriers such as metal–organic frameworks [38,39], mesoporous materials [40–42], liposomes [43,44], and niosomes [45,46] have been developed for targeted drug delivery. Liposomes have advantages over these carriers such as biocompatibility, self-assembly capacity, large drug molecule loading, and different controllable physicochemical properties for various biological conditions. Niosome (Nio) is a vesicular structure made of nonionic surfactants that can be a superior substitute for liposomes [47–50]. Recently, niosomes attracted researchers’ attention due to their huge capacity as carriers in gene and drug delivery systems, their ability to protect proteins, genes, drugs, and phytochemicals against enzymatic detection and rapid clearance from systematic circulation [51–54]. High treatment efficiency and reducing drug dosage and side effects can be obtained by loading various therapeutic materials such as drugs, photothermal agents, genes, etc., in niosomes as cargo for targeted multi-treatment.

In this work, a novel targeted thermo-sensitive niosome (TS-Nio) containing CQDs as a photothermal and labeling agent and Dox as a common cancer drug was constructed. The targeted TS niosomes were designed based on phospholipid addition to a typical niosome formulation and folic acid on the outer layer. Niosomes had the photothermal effect and NIR-controllable drug release due to the loaded CQDs.

Citric acid, urea, sorbitan monopalmitate (Span 40), polyoxyethylene sorbitan monopalmitate (Tween 40), lecithin, cholesterol, folic acid (FA), N-(3- dimethyl aminopropyl)-N-ethyl carbo di imide hydrochloride (EDC), doxorubicin hydrochloride (Dox), ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA), dialysis bag (MWCO = 12,000 Da and 8,000 Da), trypsin-EDTA solution, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), high glucose DMEM and RPMI medium were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, United States,).

The size distribution of the synthesized CQDs and nanocarriers was studied by Zetasizer NanoZS90 at 25 °C (Malvern Instruments ltd, Malvern, Worcestershire). The morphology of the CQDs was studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi, Japan). The surface functional groups were characterized by Bruker TENSOR 27 FTIR spectrophotometer (Bruker Optics GmbH, Ettlingen, Germany). The optical fluorescence and absorption spectra were studied by Varian Cary Eclipse Spectrofluorometer and Varian Cary 50 UV–vis spectrophotometer, respectively (Varian Co., Australia). Formazan was measured using an ELISA microplate reader spectrophotometer (ELx808, BioTek Instruments, Winooski, USA).

Carbon quantum dots were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. 2.5 g urea and 0.5 g citric acid were dissolved in 12 mL distilled water. The obtained clear solution was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave at 160 °C for 12 h. The solution was cooled to room temperature and was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 30 min.

Niosome was prepared by the thin-film hydration method (TFH). Cholesterol and nonionic surfactants (Tween 60 and Span 40) were dissolved in chloroform and mixed in a round-bottomed flask. The final concentrations were reached 300 μM for each formulation. In order to obtain thermo-sensitive niosome, 20% lecithin was added to the nonionic surfactant mixture. Chloroform was evaporated under vacuum at 60 °C by a rotary evaporator for 30 min. The hydration of the thin film was performed with shaking at 60 °C for 30 min with 5 mL of: deionized water, Dox (5 mg/mL), colloidal CQD, or colloidal CQD & Dox. Small unilamellar niosomes were produced after sonication of the multi-lamellar vesicles obtained from the TFH method for 15 min. Free CQD and Dox were separated from Dox @ Nio, CQD @ Nio, and CQD & Dox @ Nio by centrifugation at 15000 rpm for 20 min. The prepared niosomes were passing across a 200 nm filtration member to achieve the uniform niosomes. For FA-Nio conjugation, EDC was added to the niosomes in the mole ratio of 13:1 of the total lipid and shacked for 6 h at room temperature. 0.3 mol FA was added to the carboxyl activated niosomes, shacked at room temperature overnight, and centrifuged at 15000 rpm for an hour. The supernatant was discarded, and the precipitate was placed at room temperature overnight and then stored at 4 °C.

Entrapment efficiency (EE) of the prepared TS-Nio was calculated during the hydration of thin film by shaking with 5 mL of deionized water containing Dox or CQD & Dox at 60 °C for 30 min. Dox concentration in the supernatant was calculated after centrifugation at 15000 rpm using electronic absorption at 480 nm.

2 mL of Dox @ TS-Nio-FA was transferred to an 8 kDa dialysis bag, and placed in 50 mL of PBS solution under different conditions (temperature: 37 °C and 45 °C, pH: 5.2 and 7.4). The beaker was stirred with a magnetic stirrer at 80 rpm. At predetermined times, 2 mL of released medium was removed and replaced with fresh PBS. The released Dox was measured at 480 nm by a UV–vis spectrophotometer. Each experiment was repeated three times. For IR-triggered drug release investigation, two series of these experiments (in triplicate) were repeated with CQD & Dox @ TS-Nio-FA at different conditions (temperature: 37 °C and 45 °C, pH: 5.2 and 7.4). One series was irradiated with 808 nm laser for 10 min, and the other series was without laser irradiation. The amount of released Dox was determined.

SK-BR-3, MDA-MB-231, and MCF-7 as human breast cancer cell lines and MCF-10 as a normal human mammary epithelial cell line were cultured in high glucose DMEM medium with 10% (v/v) heat inactive FBS, and 1% penicillin/streptomycin solution in 25 cm2 flask in the presence of 5% CO2 at 37 °C to reaching 80% confluence. During the cell culture studies, Trypsin/EDTA were used for the cell passage two times a week.

The cytotoxicity of CQDs, Nio, TS-Nio, Nio-FA, and TS-Nio-FA and the chemotherapy effect of Dox, Dox @ TS-Nio, and Dox @ Nio on MCF-7 cells were investigated by a conventional MTT test in a dose-dependent way according to our previous reports [1,2,55]. Briefly, 1 × 104 cells were incubated in every well of 96 well plate and incubated overnight in the standard cell culture condition. The culture medium was replaced with the fresh medium, including various concentrations of formulations for 24 h. Every concentration has three duplications. At the end of incubation, cells were washed with PBS and treated with 100 μL of 0.5 mg/mL MTT solution (in culture medium) for four hours. The produced formazan crystals were dissolved in 100 μL DMSO during the shacking of the mixture. Finally, the optical density of each well was recorded using the spectrometric microplate reader at 570 nm.

SK-BR-3, MDA-MB-231, MCF-7, and MCF-10 cells were seeded into 96-well cell culture plates for 24 h, and treated with formulations in the presence and absence of Dox. In PTT tests, they were irradiated with an 808 nm NIR laser for 10 min and incubated for 22 h. The culture medium was removed, and cells were washed with PBS. Cell viability was determined by the MTT method.

CQDs as fluorescence and photothermal agent were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal reaction of citric acid and urea as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively. The other nitrogen sources such as 2–2, ethylene dioxy bis ethylamine, ethylene diamine, and diethylenetriamine were used for CQDs synthesized, but they did not show any absorption in the NIR range. A series of characterization methods were applied to the synthesized CQDs. Through the dynamic light scattering, it was found that the CQDs were monodispersed in various environments such as water, PBS, and DMEM, with an average diameter of about 2 nm. Fig. 1 shows the hydrodynamic diameter of the synthesized CQDs after 1, 3, 7 and 14 days. As the results show, they were stable after 14 days. The morphology and size distribution of the CQDs were studied by HRTEM (Fig. 1b).

FT-IR spectra of the CQDs, uric acid, and citric acid for comparison are shown in Fig. 2 a. The stretching vibration peaks of NH and OH groups of the CQDs can be observed at 3445 cm−1. The absorption band at 1641 cm−1 is related to the double bond stretching vibration of CC bonds. The absorption bands observed at 1007 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 are referred to the stretching vibration of CO and CN bonds. Fig. 2b shows the electronic absorption spectra of the CQDs, uric acid, and citric acid. The absorption peaks at 240 and 335 nm are related to the π → π* transition and n → π* transition, respectively. These two characteristic peaks confirm the CQDs formation. Absorption at the wavelength of 650 nm shows the suitability of the synthesized CQDs for use as a photothermal agent.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CQDs (Fig. 3 a) shows stable emission at 450 nm under different excitation wavelengths (300 – 360 nm), which indicates relatively uniform size and almost no surface defects. The excitation-emission spectra of the CQDs are shown in Fig. 3b. The fluorescence emission stability of CQDs was investigated at various pH and solvents. According to the Fig. 3c, the photoluminescence was enhanced with increasing pH from 3 to 7, and the maximum fluorescence intensity was observed in the neutral pH, which is suitable for biological applications [56]. On the other hand, the fluorescence stability of the CQDs was investigated in PBS, RPMI, and FBS, and no significant change was observed.

The relative measurement method was applied to calculate the quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized nanocrystals. The QY of the CQDs was obtained using the ratio of the slope of the photoluminescence intensity curves vs absorbance for six concentrations of CQDs and quinine sulfate (Ex = 310, QY = 0.54) as a reference. The slope of CQDs and QS were 4880.6 and 3923.8, respectively, and the calculated quantum yield was 0.67.

Urea plays an impressive role in the absorbance properties of the CQDs in NIR range [22]. While the synthesized CQDs using citric acid or urea do not show any electronic absorption in the NIR region, their absorption maxima shift to 630 and 650 nm by increasing the urea to citric acid molar ratio to 3 in a hydrothermal or solvothermal synthesis procedure (at 160 °C for 12 h) respectively. The synthesized CQDs by hydrothermal route using citric acid to urea molar ratio of 1:5 showed the maximum absorption at 650 nm (Fig. 4 ).

The photothermal performance of various concentrations of the CQDs (0.5–4 μg/mL) were investigated using an 808 nm laser at 1 w/cm2 during 10 min. The temperature was monitored using an infrared thermometer. Fig. 5 a shows a clear distinction between the control samples (distilled water and PBS) and different concentrations of the CQDs. These results propose that the synthesized CQDs in optimized conditions can convert laser irradiation to heat effectively and can be used as an efficient photothermal agent. Fig. 5b shows the changes in temperature of the highest concentration of the prepared CQDs during LASER on/off cycle (10 min duration for each cycle and 15-second intervals). Fig. 5c shows the regression of the cooling time versus
- ln θ
while the θ is the driving force temperature (
θ =
T -
T
surr
T
max
-
T
surr
). According to Fig. 5c, the time constant for heat transfer from the system was determined to be τs = 300 s. A corrected photothermal conversion model by thermal loss due to the natural convection was used to calculate the photothermal efficiency of CQDs [22]. Based on Fig. 5b and 5c, the photothermal conversion efficiency was calculated about 24.3%.

The photothermal stability of the CQDs as an important key property of photothermal agents is shown in Fig. 5d. According to these results, during four successive laser on/off cycles, the prepared CQDs retained their photothermal conversion performance as their resistance to photobleaching property. The photoluminescence intensity and UV–vis absorption of the CQDs were recorded and compared after four cycle’s irradiation, and no significant change was observed.

The vesicles were obtained during the conventional thin film method using a chloroform solution of nonionic surfactants, cholesterol, and phospholipid. Their size distribution and morphology of formulations were studied by optical light microscopy with a 100 × magnification lens at room temperature. The spherical shapes with about 170 nm mean diameter were observed in the micrograph that is shown in Fig. 6 a. Clearly, it was observed that there isn’t any cholesterol crystal or diosgenin. The observed circular shapes in Fig. 6 are similar to the previous reports and confirm the successful synthesis of niosomes [57,58]. The hydrodynamic diameter of niosome and TS-niosomes in various environments such as distilled water, PBS, and cell culture media with or without fetal bovine serum were investigated. The results of the DLS analysis in Table 1 show that the synthesized vesicles are stable for two weeks without significant size change.

The Dox entrapment efficiency of thermo-sensitive niosome was calculated using Dox concentration in supernatant after 30 min of shaking at 60 °C. The entrapping amount of Dox in each niosome formulation was calculated using the following equation: [59–61].
(1) EE% = (initial mass of Dox − mass of Dox in supernatant/initial mass of Dox) × 100

The doxorubicin entrapment efficiency of 85% and 82% were calculated in the presence and absence of CQDs, respectively. The results show obviously that presence of the carbon quantum dots does not have any significant effect on drug loading. The hydrophilic nature of the CQDs and doxorubicin hydrochloride causes their accumulation in the hydrophilic central cavity of TS-Nio.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal effect and in vitro release study
ce:section.ce:para:
The drug release from carrier formulations in various conditions was studied using a dialysis membrane. [62–67]. Drug releasing kinetics of the prepared formulations in various conditions of temperature and pH were studied using the following equation, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 .
(2) Release rate of Dox = mass of released Dox/mass of loaded Dox × 100%

At biological condition (pH = 7.4, temperature = 37 °C), only 28.1% and 17.4% of entrapped Dox were released from niosome and TS-Nio, respectively after 13 h while in a tumor condition and with photothermal effect (pH = 5.2, temperature = 45 °C) 49% and 72.2% of the Dox were released from niosome and TS-Nio respectively. It can be concluded that Dox can be released faster from TS niosome in tumor conditions. In biological temperature and tumor pH, 43% and 34.6% of the Dox were released from niosome and TS-Nio, respectively after 13 h, while at the normal tissue pH with laser irradiation, niosome and TS niosome were released approximately 37% and 68.3% of the loaded Dox. The statistically significant difference between the two nanocarriers is due to the presence of phospholipids in the structure of the TS niosome.

Various kinetic models, in particular zero order (eq. 3), first-order (eq. 4), Higuchi (eq.5), and Komsmeyer & Peppas (KP) (eq. 6) models, were examined to further evaluation of drug release kinetics [68].
(3) Qt = Q0 + k0 t
(4) Qt = Q0e-k 1
t k1/2.303
(5) Qt = kH.t1/2
(6) Mt/M = kkpt n
where k0, k1, kH, and kkp are the constants for zero, first, Higuchi, and Komsmeyer & Peppas model, also Q0 and Qt are the total concentration of loaded Dox and the cumulative amount of released drug at the time t, respectively. Mt/M is the fraction of drug released, and n is the diffusional exponent. Table 2 indicates that in the most of the formulations, KP model fits the experimental data best in various conditions based on the correlation coefficient. It considers erosion-controlled release and diffusion release processes from the TS niosome and niosome. In the beginning, in the presence of PBS, niosome and TS niosome might expand slightly and allow the Dox within to diffuse through the interface. In addition, the n value indicates the drug release mechanisms, so the values less than and or greater than 0.45 suggest quasi-fickian and anomalous diffusion or non-fickian diffusion mechanisms, respectively.

The cytotoxicity of the CQDs, niosome, niosome-FA, TS-Nio, and TS-Nio-FA, and chemotherapy of Dox, CQDs & Dox loaded in niosome and TS-Nio and their conjugation with folic acid were investigated by an ordinary MTT assay. The concentration-dependent toxicity of various formulations was evaluated. For this purpose, MCF-7 cells were incubated with different formulations. Dox was used at the same concentrations in each group. The cell viability was calculated according to the following equation.
(7) Cell viability rate = Absorbance of treated cells/Absorbance of control group × 100

As shown in Fig. 8 , the MTT results revealed that CQD is a safe and non-toxic photothermal and labeling agent even at high concentrations. The TS-Nio and TS-Nio-FA are more biocompatible than niosome and niosome-FA at all concentrations. Compared to the free Dox in each group, the toxicity of Dox-loaded formulations is lower than that of free Dox at all concentrations. This can be due to the released percentages of the Dox-loaded formulations in different conditions. Because only less than 15% and 30% of loaded drug in niosome and TS-Nio were released overnight in biological conditions (Fig. 7).

The IC50 value of the formulations for the MCF-7 cell line has been calculated and reported in Table 3 . Due to the fact that the maximum amount of CQDs used in the final formulation was about 1.02 × 1017, the obtained IC50 is about 15 times more than the highest required value, so CQD is completely non-toxic and biocompatible. The other key point in this work is that the IC50 of TS-Nio is about 1.5 times more than the niosome, and it proves that the designed formulation is obviously more biocompatible than the common niosomes, while the mortality rate in TS-Nio is significantly higher with the presence of Dox, which indicates more entry into the cancer cells.

To clarify the effect of NIR laser irradiation, chemotherapy and chemo-photothermal therapy on cell viability, the MDA-MB-231 cells were used as a model for cellular imaging in various conditions. As shown in Fig. 10 b, the NIR laser irradiation has no photothermal damage on the cells in high glucose DMEM medium without CQDs. The biocompatibility of CQDs is obviously clear with the absence of cell deformation in Fig. 10c; however, after laser irradiation of cells containing CQDs, about 50% of the cells were destroyed (Fig. 10d). The CQDs & Dox @ TS-Nio-FA kill less than 50% of the cells even after 24 h (Fig. 10e), while NIR irradiation of cells incubated with CQDs & Dox @ TS-Nio-FA led to notable chemo-photothermal ablation about 90% of MDA-MB-231 cells (Fig. 10f). The results indicate that the CQD & drug @ TS-Nio as an effective drug carrier can simplify combined chemotherapy and photothermal therapy to provide high therapeutic efficacy.

In this work, a novel NIR-sensitive niosome was introduced, which contains carbon quantum dots and Dox as photothermal agent and anticancer drug, respectively. Rapid Dox release from the CQD & Dox @ TS-Nio-FA could be readily achieved in comparison with common niosome formulations upon NIR laser irradiation, which might be mainly due to the phase transition and the enhancement of permeability of TS-Nio membrane in effect of the heat diffusion from CQDs loaded in the formulation. The MTT assay results indicate that the treatment of various breast cancer cells with CQD & Dox @ TS-Nio-FA followed by laser irradiation display significantly more toxicity than the treatment with Dox, Dox @ TS-Nio-FA, and Dox @ Nio-FA (chemotherapy) and CQD plus laser irradiation (photothermal therapy). Increase intracellular Dox concentration as a result of the targeted and triggered release of Dox from CQD & Dox @ TS-Nio-FA due to the overexpressed folate receptors on the cancerous cells surface, the photothermal effect, and a synergistic effect of photothermal and drug cytotoxicity effect are the main reasons for the significant increase in the cytotoxicity effect of CQD & Dox @ TS-Nio-FA plus NIR laser irradiation. According to the results, the designed targeted TS-Nio as an effective, efficient, biocompatible and cost-effective carrier that can have a significant impact on the treatment of cancer cells.

Faeze Khakbaz: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Mohammad Mirzaei: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mohamad Mahani: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Micro-RNA detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer of DNA-carbon quantum dots probes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Khakbaz, M. Mahani, Micro-RNA detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer of DNA-carbon quantum dots probes, Analytical biochemistry, 523 (2017) 32-38.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A carbon dot and molecular beacon based fluorometric sensor for the cancer marker microRNA-21

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Mahani, Z. Mousapour, F. Divsar, A. Nomani, H. Ju, A carbon dot and molecular beacon based fluorometric sensor for the cancer marker microRNA-21, Microchimica Acta, 186 (2019) 132.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-embedded N-acetylneuraminic acid and glucuronic acid-imprinted polymers for targeting and imaging of cancer cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Mahani, F. Mahmoudi, J. Fassihi, Z. Hasani, F. Divsar, Carbon dots-embedded N-acetylneuraminic acid and glucuronic acid-imprinted polymers for targeting and imaging of cancer cells, Microchimica Acta, 188 (2021) 1-11.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Doxorubicin delivery to breast cancer cells with transferrin-targeted carbon quantum dots: An in vitro and in silico study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Mahani, M. Pourrahmani-Sarbanani, M. Yoosefian, F. Divsar, S.M. Mousavi, A. Nomani, Doxorubicin delivery to breast cancer cells with transferrin-targeted carbon quantum dots: An in vitro and in silico study, Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology, 62 (2021) 102342.
